pairs.question: Which candidate won the 1912 United States presidential election in South Dakota?
pairs.answer: Theodore Roosevelt, the Republican nominee, won the 1912 United States presidential election in South Dakota.

pairs.question: What was the margin of victory for Theodore Roosevelt over Woodrow Wilson in the 1912 South Dakota election?
pairs.answer: Theodore Roosevelt defeated Woodrow Wilson by a margin of 8.49% in the 1912 South Dakota election.

pairs.question: Why did the South Dakota Republican Party nominate electors for Theodore Roosevelt instead of William Howard Taft in 1912?
pairs.answer: The South Dakota Republican Party, led by progressives and influenced by Senator Coe I. Crawford, preferred Theodore Roosevelt over Taft. Despite Taft winning the national Republican nomination, South Dakota's Republicans nominated Roosevelt's electors, leading to controversy with conservative Taft supporters.

pairs.question: Was William Howard Taft on the ballot or available as a write-in option in South Dakota during the 1912 election?
pairs.answer: No, William Howard Taft was neither on the ballot nor available as a write-in option in South Dakota during the 1912 election. South Dakota was the only state in 1912 without Taft on the ballot or as a write-in.

pairs.question: What percentage of the popular vote did Theodore Roosevelt receive in South Dakota during the 1912 election?
pairs.answer: Theodore Roosevelt received 50.56% of the popular vote in South Dakota during the 1912 election, making it his strongest state in terms of popular vote percentage.

pairs.question: Was South Dakota the only non-former Confederate state to give any candidate an absolute majority of the vote in the 1912 election?
pairs.answer: Yes, South Dakota was the only non-former Confederate state to give any candidate an absolute majority of the vote in the 1912 election.

pairs.question: How did Senator Robert J. Gamble predict the outcome of the 1912 South Dakota election would be affected by conservative Republicans?
pairs.answer: Senator Robert J. Gamble predicted that conservative Republicans, angered by the selection of Roosevelt's electors, would support Woodrow Wilson to defeat Roosevelt. Despite this, Roosevelt still carried the state, though with a reduced margin compared to Taft's 1908 victory.
